“The Only Constant in Life is Change.”

a  —Heraclitus
The advent of large language models (LLMs) has
revolutionized code generation (Jiang et al., 2024;
Zheng et al., 2023; Fan et al., 2023), with modern
tools like GitHub Copilot and ChatGPT demon-
strating remarkable capabilities in synthesizing
functionally correct code. Benchmarks like Hu-
manEval (Chen et al., 2021a), MBPP (Odena et al.,
2021), and SWE-Bench (Jimenez et al., 2023) have
driven these advancements by measuring correct-
ness through static test cases. However, this narrow

focus overlooks a critical dimension of real-world
software engineering: maintainability—the ability
to adapt code to evolving requirements with mini-
mal rework.
Maintainability Crisis  Heraclitus’ philosophy,
"The only constant in life is change," reflects a
truth painfully evident in software evolution: as
user needs evolve, markets shift, and technologies
advance, software undergo perpetual changes. This
oversight on maintainability leads to the dangerous
reality—code that works today but becomes pro-
hibitively expensive to adapt tomorrow (Blischke
et al., 2003). For instance, the collapse of Knight
Capital exemplifies this crisis—$440 million lost in
minutes due to unmaintained legacy code (Saltapi-
das and Maghsood, 2018); the $100 billion reme-
diation cost for Year 2000 Problem revealed how
short-sighted design decisions create exponential
future costs (Reps et al., 1997).  Industry studies
also quantifies the severity: 60–80% of software
lifecycle costs stem from post-deployment mainte-
nance (Ogheneovo et al., 2014; Banker et al., 1993),
primarily due to structural deficiencies like high
coupling and low cohesion that amplify rework
effort.
Motivation  Although recent benchmarks have
expanded evaluations to diverse dimensions like
readability and efficiency (Zheng et al., 2024;
Huang et al., 2024), yet retain static test cases. The
static test cases fails to capture the iterative nature
of software evolution, and are ill-suited for main-
tainability evaluation. Moreover, traditional soft-
ware metrics like cyclomatic complexity and main-
tainability index, still remain limited to static code
analysis, failing to capture the dynamic scenarios.
Meanwhile, code generation systems—from foun-
dation models like CodeLLaMA (Roziere et al.,
2023) to multi-agent frameworks like SWE-agent
(Yang et al., 2024)—just optimize for task com-
pletion but neglect structural qualities critical for

Code Generation Benchmarks  Existing  re-
search on code generation has been propelled by
benchmarks emphasizing functional correctness.
Pioneering works span multiple levels of complex-
ity: function-level tasks, such as HumanEval (Chen
et al., 2021b) and MBPP (Austin et al., 2021);
competition-level problems, as addressed by APPS
(Hendrycks et al., 2021) and CodeContests (Li
et al., 2022); and repository-scale challenges tack-
led by SWE-Bench (Jimenez et al., 2023) and Re-
poBench (Liu et al., 2023). Despite these advance-
ments, these benchmarks predominantly use static
test cases measuring correctness through execution
pass rates. Although recent efforts, such as RACE
(Zheng et al., 2024) and EffiBench (Huang et al.,
2024), have expanded the evaluation to include
readability and efficiency, yet remain constrained
by static evaluation, failing to capture the dynamic
nature of software development. MaintainBench
bridges this critical gap with metricization of code
maintainability through requirement evolution cy-
cles, where 80% of software costs occur in real-
world applications (Ogheneovo et al., 2014; Banker
et al., 1993; Bennett and Rajlich, 2000).
Code Generation Systems  Modern code gen-
eration systems have evolved from three key as-
pects: foundation models, instruction-tuned vari-
ants, and multi-agent frameworks.  Foundation
models such as AlphaCode (Li et al., 2022) and
CodeLLaMA (Roziere et al., 2023), established
fundamental code synthesis capabilities through
large-scale pretraining on code corpora.  Build-
ing on these foundations, instruction-tuned vari-
ants like WizardCoder (Luo et al., 2023) and Magi-
coder (Wei et al., 2024) improved context under-
standing through data distillation and complex in-
struction augmentation. The frontier now shifts to
multi-agent frameworks: MetaGPT (Hong et al.,
2023) employs standardized outputs to coordinate
multiple agents, while SWE-agent (Yang et al.,
2024) and OpenDevin (Wang et al., 2024) focus on
repository-level task decomposition. AgentCoder
(Huang et al., 2023) introduces specialized testing
agents, and ReflectionCoder (Ren et al., 2024) im-
plements iterative self-improvement mechanisms.
However, all of them overlook maintainability, and
are prone to generate brittle code that is difficult
to maintain. MaintainCoder bridges this gap by
integrating principles like the waterfall model and
classical design patterns.

3.1  Problem Formulation
Task Definition Modern code generation sys-
tems optimize for  immediate  task correctness
but neglect software engineering’s core challenge:
evolving requirements {R1, ..., Rn} drive itera-
tive code adaptations {C1, ..., Cn}, where each
Ci |= Ri incurs maintenance cost M(Ci−1 → Ci).
Current approaches treat each Ri independently, ig-
noring structural deficiencies that accrue technical
debt. We investigate maintainable code generator G
producing C1 = G(R1) with both high correctness
and maintainability.
Maintainability Measurement  Previous works
measure maintainability through static analysis in-
dexes, such as Halstead volume (Hariprasad et al.,
2017), cyclomatic complexity or maintainability in-
dex. However, the ultimate goal of maintainability
is to reduce maintenance costs. We thus formal-
ize dynamic maintainability through cumulative
maintenance costs with discount factor γ.

Given maintainability’s intrinsic nature, we posit
that limited probing can substitute unlimited re-
quirement evolution simulations.  We employ
Monte Carlo estimation and first-order truncation
as the proxy to address infinite expectation horizons
and persistent requirement changes, which reduces
both computational cost and estimation variance,
yielding:

Specifically, maintenance costs embody in: (1)
functional correctness post-modification, (2) lines-
of-code change magnitude, and (3) abstract syntax
tree similarity between versions.
3.2 MaintainBench: A Dynamic Benchmark
for Code Maintainability
In this section, we introduce MaintainBench, a
novel benchmark designed to evaluate code main-
tainability in response to evolving software re-
quirements.  Unlike traditional code generation
benchmarks that focus solely on correctness, Main-
tainBench evaluates how effectively models can
adapt existing code to meet changing requirements.
3

MaintainBench consists of five carefully curated
datasets: HumanEval-Ext, MBPP-Ext, APPS-Ext,
CodeContest-Ext, and xCodeEval-Ext, comprising
over 500 Python programming data of three dif-
fculty levels. Each extends established code gen-
eration benchmarks with systematic requirement
changes. The dataset construction process consists
of three key stages: (1) Data Selection and Prepro-
cessing, (2) Extended Data Construction, and (3)
Data Quality Check. The overview of this process
is shown in Fig. 1.
3.2.1  Data Selection and Preprocessing
MaintainBench consists of three different diffi-
culty levels of code:  entry-level, mixture-level,
and competition-level. Specifically, it extends five
widely used code generation benchmarks: Hu-
manEval (Chen et al., 2021a), MBPP (Odena et al.,
2021), APPS (Hendrycks et al., 2021), xCodeEval
(Khan et al., 2023), and CodeContests (Li et al.,
2022).
Entry Level  For entry-level difficulty, we select
problems from the HumanEval and MBPP datasets,
which are designed to be solvable by newbie pro-
grammers. These datasets are widely used to evalu-
ate the code generation capabilities of LLMs, cov-
ering topics such as language comprehension, al-
gorithms, basic mathematics, programming funda-
mentals, and standard library functionality. Each
problem includes a task description, a reference
solution, and a set of automated test cases. We
sample 30 problems from each dataset, which we
then extend to 120 new problems by systematically
modifying their requirements.
Mixture Level  For mixture-level difficulty, we
extend the APPS dataset, which contains problems
ranging from introductory to collegiate competition
levels. This dataset evaluates both coding profi-
ciency and problem-solving ability. We begin with
a subset of 50 problems and extend them to over
200 new problems. Furthermore, to eliminate the
format inconsistency such as keyboard input, we
employ GPT-4o to generate purely functional im-
plementations, which are then manually reviewed
and refined. We standardize the dataset by ensuring
that the original solution is expressed exclusively
in functional form, while the original test cases are
converted into Python assertions.
Competition  Level  The  competition-level
dataset includes problems from CodeContests and

programming tasks, while xCodeEval provides
a  large-scale executable dataset with varying
levels of difficulty. To distinguish this level from
entry-level and mixture-level tasks, we select 30
high-difficulty problems from each dataset and
extend them to over 120 new problems. As with
the mixture-level dataset, we standardize the data
format using LLM-generated solutions, followed
by manual verification to ensure correctness and
consistency.
3.2.2  Extended Data Generation
Building on our preprocessed dataset, the core in-
novation of MaintainBench is the systematic appli-
cation of four requirement change patterns, formal-
ized as:

where P0, S0, and T0, represent the original prob-

applies one of four requirements change patterns

, and generates an initial extended versions. The re-
finement process ensures that the adapted solution
remains correct by validating it against an updated
test suite T1, ultimately yielding the final reference
solution and test cases for the modified require-
ment πi. This transformation process is designed

h. 1) Data preprocessing selects and curates problems
el, and competition-level difficulties.  2) Requirement
tional extensions, interface modifications, data structure
te evolved problem variants. 3) Solution-test co-evolution
e execution and refinement. 4) Quality check employs
an expert review to ensure reliability, correctness, and
to simulate realistic software evolution scenarios
where developers must modify existing code to
accommodate changing requirements rather than
implementing solutions from scratch.
Requirement Change Generation We craft four
distinct requirement change patterns to capture soft-
ware evolution scenarios. As detailed:
1) Functional Extensions[πext]: Functional ex-
tensions increase complexity by introducing addi-
tional programming challenges while preserving
the core functionality and real-world relevance of
the original problem. Effective solutions must both
comprehend the original code and appropriately ex-
tend its problem-solving capabilities. For example,
the extended problem should involve self-invoking
(Yu et al., 2024), modeling calls to the original
functions, and maintaining semantic consistency.
2) Interface Modifications[πint]: Interface mod-
ifications further enhance the diversity of gener-
ated problems, and examine the adaptability of
original solutions in protocol dimension. E.g., the
API changes caused by the update of the exter-
nal dependency library. The modified problem re-
mains closely related to the original, but introduces
changes to input parameters, return types, or other
interface components to assess the robustness and
flexibility of solutions.
3) Data Structure Transformations[πdst]: Data
structure transformations require the generated
problem to adopt different data structures where the
description explicitly specifies the modifications of
the data representation. This transformation mim-
ics real-world scenarios in which software systems

evolve to accommodate efficiency, scalability, or
compatibility constraints.
4) Error Handling Enhancements[πerr]: Error
handling enhancements need the generated prob-
lem to remain relevant to the original while intro-
ducing explicitly required error-handling mecha-
nisms in the solution. This includes defining spe-
cific error types such as NameError, SyntaxError,
TypeError, and other domain-specific exceptions.
Through these enhancements, the problem more
accurately reflects a fundamental aspect of the reli-
ability and maintainability of the system.
Solution-Test Co-evolution  Generally, we em-
ploy GPT-4o to generate initial versions of each
′
transformation, including preliminary solution S1
′
and test cases T1, followed by an iterative refine-
ment process as validation.

In particular, we ex-
′  ′
ecute S1 against T1 using a Python interpreter to
verify correctness. For any failing tests, we con-
duct manual expert reviews to identify and resolve
issues in both the solution and test cases, transform-
′  ′
ing S1 into S1 and T1 into T1. If problems persist,
we repeat the execution-review cycle until all test
cases pass successfully.
Our extension methodology maintains coherence
between solutions and test cases through an orches-
trated co-generation process.  Instead of generat-
ing these components independently, which could
lead to misalignment or hallucination, we derive
extended solutions by adapting original code ac-
cording to specific requirement change patterns,
then generate corresponding test cases to verify
both preserved and new functionality. Through this
linked generation, we ensure a traceable evolution-
ary path with minimal, intentional changes directly
responsive to requirements.
3.2.3  Quality Check
To ensure a high quality and reliability of Main-
tainBench, we further implement a quality check
process for both code solutions and test cases. First,
we review LLM-generated problem descriptions to
ensure they accurately correspond to the intended
extension criteria. Second, programming experts
correct identified issues, focusing on alignment
between problem descriptions, test cases, and re-
quirement changes. Finally, a separate review team
performs cross-validation to verify functionality
preservation, correct implementation of new re-
quirements, minimally scoped modifications, and
comprehensive test coverage. The details of quality
5

check are provided in Appendix A.1
3.3 MaintainCoder: Multi-Agent System for
Maintainable Code Generation
MaintainCoder addresses the challenge of main-
tainable code generation via a novel multi-agent
system that mirrors the human software develop-
ment lifecycle. It designs an orchestrated pipeline
of LLM agents, each specializing in distinct phases
of software development while maintaining contex-
tual awareness through inter-agent communication
empowered by AutoGen (Wu et al., 2023).
System Overview MaintainCoder features best
practice like the Waterfall model, classical design
patterns, and iterative refinement mechanisms for
critical components. As illustrated in Fig. 2, Main-
tainCoder operates through two principal modules:
Code Framework and Code Generation. The pro-
cess initiates with requirement analysis and culmi-
nates in optimized code generation through well-
structured phases: requirements analysis, design
pattern selection, framework design, framework
evaluation, code generation,  test case insertion,
code execution, code optimization, etc.
3.3.1  Code Framework Module
This module transforms user requirements into
maintainable architectural blueprints.
Requirements Analysis Agent  The  require-
ments analysis agent is tasked with in-depth analy-
sis of software requirements within MaintainCoder.
It decomposes the problem step by step through
chain-of-thoughts, prioritizing user goals and prac-
tical constraints. The agent receives plain text doc-
uments describing software requirements, extracts
key requirements, identifies core functions, high-
lights key challenges, and proposes high-level solu-
tions where applicable. The output analysis report
is concise, covering core functions and key chal-
lenges, and provides clear guidance and support for
the follow-up agents. This ensures that the analysis
process avoids unnecessary complexity and focuses
on high-priority tasks and computational logic.
Design Pattern Selection Agent  The design pat-
tern selection agent acts as the software architect
within MaintainCoder, focusing on selecting appro-
priate design patterns for each functional module.
It receives functional modules and key challenges
from the requirements analysis agent, evaluates the
overall architecture and module interactions, and

Framework Design Agent  The framework de-
sign agent is responsible for building a modular
and robust code framework. Based on provided
functional modules and design patterns, the agent
not only designs a preliminary class structure en-
suring each class focuses on a single responsibility,
but also clarifies dependencies to improve code
modularity, reusability, and maintainability to fu-
ture requirements. When receiving modification

4.1  Experimental Procedure
The experiment aims to evaluate the performance
of the MaintainCoder framework on the task of
generating highly maintainable code. We use the
MaintainBench dataset proposed in this work. The
experiment consists of two steps. First, we gen-
erate the initial code C0 for the original prob-
lem P0 using MaintainCoder and several baseline
models. We then evaluate and record the metrics

of the initial code. Next, based on C0, we uni-
formly apply GPT-4o-mini to generate modified
versions of the code with different types of changes:
C0 → {Cext, Cint, Cdst, Cerr}. We measure and
record the metrics of the modified code in the sec-
ond round.
4.2  Baselines
To demonstrate the effectiveness of MaintainCoder,
we compare it against several baseline models, in-
cluding GPT-4o-mini, Claude-3.7-Sonnet, Qwen-
Plus, and DeepSeek-V3.  Additionally, we ana-
lyze two extended GPT techniques: COT and Self-
Planning. COT enhances reasoning capabilities
through chain-of-thought prompting, while Self-
Planning emphasizes planning abilities in code gen-
eration tasks.
4.3 Metrics
Static Metrics  The static metrics include the
Maintainability Index (MI) and Cyclomatic Com-
plexity (CC). MI is calculated based on factors
such as lines of code, comment ratio, cyclomatic
complexity, and Halstead complexity, providing
a comprehensive perspective on the static main-
tainability of the code from multiple dimensions,

including complexity and readability. CC, as part
of the MI, measures the number of independent
paths in the control flow graph, reflecting the logi-
cal complexity of the code. A higher CC indicates
more branches and conditional paths, making the
code more complex.
Dynamic Metrics  The dynamic metrics  in-
clude: Post-modification functional correctness**
(pass@k); Code change volume** (Codediff ),
measured by the percentage and absolute number
of modified lines; Syntax tree similarity** before
and after modification (Astsim).
Experimental Settings  For all experiments, we
set Temperature = 0.3 and top_p = 0.95. To enhance
stability, we measure pass@5.
5  Results
5.1 Main Experiments
In this section, we evaluate the performance of
MaintainCoder on MaintainBench for the main-
tainable code generation task. Overall, Maintain-
Coder exhibits superior performance across dif-
ferent dataset difficulty levels, with its advantage

In this paper, we introduce the MaintainCoder
framework and the MaintainBench benchmark for
more maintainable code generation.  Maintain-
Bench, the first benchmark dedicated to maintain-
ability evaluation, covers diverse code modifica-
tion scenarios and provides a standardized test-
ing platform for future research. Experimental re-
sults demonstrate that MaintainCoder significantly
outperforms existing methods on MaintainBench,
comprehensively improves both static and dynamic
maintainability metrics by 14–30%, while holding
even higher functional correctness, validating its
effectiveness and robustness.
Future Work  This work introduces a new per-
spective on maintainability evaluation in code gen-
eration and establishes MaintainBench as a repro-
ducible benchmark for future studies. Future work
will focus on expanding MaintainBench with larger
scale and more diverse problem types to further ver-
ify the framework’s generality and stability. We

ginal problem of MaintainBench

also plan to explore novel generation strategies and

Contents
A.1 Manually Quality Check  .  .  .  .  .  12
A.1 Manually Quality Check
Our approach combines automated validation with
expert human review across multiple stages:
• In  the first  stage, we verify  that LLM-
generated descriptions accurately correspond
to our specified extension criteria. Once vali-
dated, we derive extended solutions by adapt-
ing original code according to specific require-
ment change patterns,  then generate corre-
sponding test cases to verify both preserved
and new functionality.
• In the second stage, programming experts re-
view and correct identified issues after gener-
ating the whole benchmark, focusing on align-
ing problem descriptions, test cases, and the
intended requirement changes. Particularly,
they verify that test cases effectively exercise
all implementation aspects (e.g., exception
handlers in error handling cases) and that solu-
tions properly reuse original code rather than
introducing unnecessary rewrites, which aims
to maintain the semantic integrity of func-
tional extensions for modified components.
• In the third stage, a separate team of review-
ers performs cross-validation between original
and modified implementations to verify that:
(1) core functionality is preserved across all
variants, (2) new requirements are correctly
implemented, (3) code modifications are ap-
propriately scoped and minimal, and (4) test
cases provide comprehensive coverage of both
original and new functionality.
